title: Hooking a simple geometric rug
subtitle: A wool-hooked rug with a bold geometric design on hessian backing, using a traditional rug hook
excerpt: Rug hooking pulls loops of cut wool strips through a hessian or linen backing using a blunt hook. A geometric design (squares, triangles, or chevrons) is the most manageable for a first rug because the colour boundaries are straight lines and the fill areas are large. A finished rug approximately 40 x 60 cm requires 200-300 g of wool strips.
sourceNotes: Traditional rug hooking on hessian is documented in Jennie Rayment, Rug Hooking (various editions) and in UK rug hooking guild instruction. The technique is a continuous tradition in UK craft since the late 19th century.

glossaryTerms.definition: The height of the wool loops pulled above the surface of the backing in rug hooking. Standard loop height is approximately 6-8 mm, enough to create a dense, durable pile surface. Loops pulled too short will pull back through the backing over time; loops too tall will compress unevenly and mat down.

Strips of wool cloth cut to a consistent width for rug hooking. Strip width determines loop density and texture: narrow strips (3-4 mm) give fine detail; wide strips (8-10 mm) fill large areas quickly and give a bold, thick texture. Strips are cut across the grain (from selvedge to selvedge) for consistent stretch and easier hooking.

content.text: Rug hooking is a rhythm-based craft: once the motion of pulling loops is established, the work progresses quickly.
at 6-7 mm width are the most versatile starting point for a geometric rug. Consistent
produces a flat, even pile surface that wears well under foot.

items.name: Hessian or linen rug backing, 50 x 70 cm minimum
Wool fabric or blanket, washed, in 3-4 colours
Rug hooking frame or embroidery hoop, at least 40 cm
Permanent marker for drawing the design
Non-slip rug backing or latex backing medium

content.text: Step 1: Draw and mount the design

Draw a simple geometric design on the hessian with a permanent marker: a grid of squares in two alternating colours, or chevrons in three colours. Leave a 5 cm unhooked border around all edges for finishing. Mount the hessian in the frame, pulling it taut and even.

Cut wool fabric into strips approximately 6-7 mm wide and 25-30 cm long. Cut across the grain (parallel to the selvedge) for consistent stretch. Sort by colour. Machine-washed wool is easier to hook than raw wool: the slight felting makes the strips stiffer and easier to pull into loops.

Hold a wool strip beneath the hessian. Push the hook through a hole in the hessian from above, catch the strip, and pull a loop up through the hole to about 7-8 mm height. Move one hole across and repeat, pulling a new loop. Each strip end must be pulled up through the hessian surface and trimmed level with the loops. Hook in rows following your design lines, filling each colour area completely before moving to the next.

Fold the 5 cm border allowance to the back of the rug and stitch down with strong thread. Apply a thin coat of latex backing medium to the back of the rug and allow to dry completely before use. The latex stabilises the loops and prevents them pulling out.

items.problem: Loops pull back through the hessian when adjacent loops are hooked
items.cause: Loops pulled too short or hessian weave too open for the strip width
items.fix: Pull each loop to at least 6-7 mm above the surface. Try narrower strips (5-6 mm) if the hessian holes are small.

items.problem: The rug surface is uneven with some areas higher than others
items.cause: Inconsistent loop heights across the piece
items.fix: After completing a section, press the loops flat with the palm and look for high spots. Adjust by gently pulling individual loops down from beneath. Use a consistent pulling motion rather than estimating height by feel.
